Modern smart phones and cell connected devices provide the best way access email during emergencies. Cell networks are often buffered from the disruptions power and are less susceptible wind damage than cable, phone and fiber networks. When the worst does happen cellular services are some the first systems restored. Microsoft Outlook for IOS and Android provides modern interface with functionality superior either Suite Gmail Office 365 Exchange Online. Inbox Google also provides updated user experience compared native phone apps.

Most social networking services like SnapChat, Twitter and Facebook have wide adoption, but are not safe for FERPA and HIPAA content. These services may used for some purposes, but great care would need taken not expose sensitive information, such who course. Consider using Yammer, which provides FERPA and HIPAA-aligned environment.
Skype for Business, which both HIPAA- and FERPA-aligned, allows you conduct chat with participants and meeting with 250 participants. Learn more here.
Zoom makes easy for faculty, staff, and students participate group video chat during online class sessions, host virtual office hours, collaborate remotely research, share screens and host real-time video conversations, host live web broadcasts thousands people worldwide and record the cloud computer for easy sharing. For more information Zoom free accounts and Zoom Pro accounts and differences between the two learn more here.
Google Hangouts works with people time. While you can invite many people you want join hangout, you can't have more than people hangout once, while others will get stuck hanging lobby. Hangouts FERPA-aligned, but does not have the BAA required HIPAA. Hangouts feature Suite. For more about how Hangouts works, see Google's page about Google Hangouts. Both Google Hangouts and Skype for Business are good tools for chats and conferencing.
Turn your voicemail box into telephone hotline. Simply update your greeting with timely information about the status your course(s) campus operations. Contact Information Technology (help@uw.edu) create enhanced hotline functionality such call menus.
The Canvas Learning Management system offers conference feature, available every course, that you can use meet live with students. Conferences can accommodate people. Learn more.
You can also use Zoom Skype for Business. Zoom unifies cloud video conferencing, simple online meetings, group messaging, and conference room solution into one easy-to-use platform. For more information Zoom you can learn more here. Skype for Business (formerly known "Lync"), which both HIPAA- and FERPA-aligned, allows you conduct chat with participants and meeting with 250 participants. Learn more here.
Panopto cloud-based lecture capture service. Panopto lets instructors record and publish lectures and other class activities online. Students can then view (and review) the published materials using Web browser mobile device. You can use Panopto two modes: pre-record and then post video lectures, broadcast live lecture (with three-second delay). The first mode allows you upload supplemental video content and add notes your course materials. Panopto not meant used conferencing tool. find out more about Panopto, visit the Panopto Help Center.
Canvas Student Groups are like smaller version your course and are used collaborative tool where students can work together group projects and assignments. Learn more.
Every Canvas course includes the ability create and review quizzes. Learn more about creating and managing quizzes Canvas. Learn more about creating quizzes Canvas.
The Canvas Gradebook helps instructors easily record student scores online, track student progress and calculate final grades. Grades for each assignment can calculated points, percentages, complete incomplete, pass fail, GPA scale, and letter grades. Provide secure access co-instructors and TAs view and edit student grades. Publish scores for students view securely online. Learn more.
GradePage simple interface for quarterly grade submission, can used GradeBook users, Canvas users and those who manage their grades using other software traditional approach. Learn more.
Methods are available for accessing campus materials from your personal laptop home computer. First check with your departmental support staff; your department may already have system place for accessing files from home.
Most faculty connect the network from home through their Internet Service Provider (ISP). Learn more. Connecting the Network will allow you access most your class resources and tools from home the event that you need conduct your class remotely.
The preferred method connecting through Husky OnNet (based the BIG-IP platform) which provides individual users with secure temporary connection the network from remote locations. Husky OnNet for MacOS and Husky OnNet for Windows are available for download from UWare. Self-supported Android, iOS,and other BIG-IP apps are available from third-party app stores.
Remote desktop access allows you access your work computer using another computer with connection the Internet. Your icons, programs and printers are all available. You can even transfer files between your systems. (For security purposes, remote desktop should only used conjunction with secure connection the network such Husky OnNet.) order use remote desktop access, your work computer will need remain (you can logged out, but the computer must not turned off) and you will need know your work computer's current address name. you need help finding your address, contact your local support staff. Find out more about this option Connecting Remotely.
Because remote desktop access requires your work computer remain when you are not work, the better option store your materials online (see "Store Your Course Materials Online" section, below) and then use Husky OnNet (see "Connect the Networks" section, below).
Contact Information Technology (help@uw.edu) regarding telephones, NetIDs, MyUW, email distribution lists, and general help with any the technologies mentioned above.
Contact the Center for Teaching and Learning (thectl@uw.edu) talk about best practices, whether out the classroom.
Contact Disability Services Office (dso@uw.edu) ensure all your students with disabilities are accommodated your technology choices.
For information about campus pandemic planning and communications during possible pandemic: Emergency Management Website, MyUW emergency announcements, UWTV, email.

original text:
surprise that President Obama chose nominate Ms. Elena Kagan for Supreme Court Justice. Kagan was the short list last year, when Obama nominated Sonia Sotomayor, and has been the obvious front-runner for this past year. confirmed, this New York maverick will the third woman serve the current court and years old, will also the youngest Supreme Court Justice history which will most likely give her the longest tenure. Not only was she the first woman represent the government Solicitor General this past year, but was also the first woman serve dean Harvard law school.
When deciding whom nominate replace the retiring Justice Stevens, President Obama was predominantly concerned with changing the ideological dynamic the current conservative court. realized that did not need steadfast leftist, but wanted someone who would able influence and persuade Justice Kennedy's swing vote away from the rightward direction the court currently prevalent under Chief Justice Roberts. Fortunately for the President, found the perfect candidate. Ms. Kagan not the typical Democratic nominee; she not extremely left, nor she the center, but somewhere between. She believes strong executive power and also active judiciary, not mere interpreter the constitution and statutes passed the legislature. This why she would most likely face opposition from both sides the Senate Judiciary confirmation, right wing conservatives and liberals alike.
Although she has impressive background law, one thing that may seen working against her her lack judicial experience. Many have been and are continuously accusing Kagan not being qualified for the position, reasoning that she has never once served judge. But whether Kagan has had judicial experience should real concern. She may have never before been working judge, but the extensive amount knowledge and experience law that she would bringing the court far exceeds any mere judicial experience.
addition representing the government court the first woman Solicitor General, she has also served Domestic Policy Aide under President Clinton, Senate Staff member under President Obama and Vice President Joe Biden, briefly worked maintaining private practice, and received degrees from Princeton, Oxford, and Harvard. She has had experience all three branches government, which more than enough qualify her for the position. quite obvious that Kagan will face battle the confirmation room, but these Senators really try evaluate both her weaknesses and her strengths, they will have tough time doubting her remarkable credentials.
true that she would the first nominee almost four decades not have had previous judicial experience, but one does not have sit judge court understand everything there know about law and the legal system. And let's face it, Kagan confirmed, will not make too much difference. She may actively express her judicial philosophy judge who legislates from the bench, but much she preaches, she will never have the power completely change the dynamics the U.S. political system. Yes, there are three separate branches government and may seem they are all equally powerful, but does not take much brain power realize the limited role Supreme Court justice. confirmed, Kagan will one nine members, giving her just enough power counter the rightward polarization the court which will allow ample space for Obama make some real changes, but not enough for her actually legislate and implement laws.
fact, nominee who believes active judiciary, opposed strict constructionist, should not feared but embraced, for just what this country needs. Like President Obama has always expressed, this country desperate need change. And hopefully the Senate confirms Obama's nomination for Ms. Elena Kagan July, some real changes can made soon October just the Supreme Court starts its next term.
Nesma Tawil second-year political science major. She can reached ntawil@uci.edu.

original text:
Thessaloniki Chania, Kavala Kerkira, Kos, Heraklion, Ioannina, Larissa, Lemnos, Mykonos, Mytilini, Rhodes, Samos and Santorini.
There are also services the Greek Aegean Islands (including Cyclades, Dodecanissa, North Aegean Sea and the Sporades).
both inexpensive and easy travel around the islands. There are ferry services numerous routes, with sailings most frequent during the summer. The main ports are Attica, Rafina and Piraeus, although there are regular sailings the islands from the smaller ports Alexandroupolis, Igoumenitsa, Patras, Kavala, Kyllini, Thessaloniki and Volos.
Tickets can bought from the shipping lines' offices located around the quaysides. major ports the larger lines have offices the city centre. There are two classes ticket Economy Class and First Class) which offer varying degrees comfort; couchette cabins can booked for the longer voyages those wishing avoid the sun. Nearly all ships have restaurant facilities. During high season wise buy tickets well before hand, inter-island travel very popular.
Routes from Piraeus: There are regular sailings the following ports: Dodecanese: Astipalaia, Chalki, Karpathos, Kassos,Kalymnos, Kastelorizo, Kos, Leros, Lipsi, Nissiros, Patmos, Rhodes, Symi and Tilos. Cyclades: Aegiali and Katapola (both Amorgos), Anafi, Donoussa, Folegandros, Heraklia, Ios, Kimolos, Koufonissia, Kythnos, Paros, Milos, Mykonos, Naxos,Santorini, Schinoussa, Serifos, Sifnos, Sikinos, Siros and Tinos. Peloponnese: Kithira, Gytheion, Hermioni, Methana, Monemvassia and Porto Heli. Saronic Gulf Islands: Aegina, Hydra, Poros and Spetses. Crete: Heraklion, Kastelli, Agios Nikolaos, and Sitia. Samos: Karlovassi and Vathi. North Eastern Aegean Islands: Chios, Evdilos (Ikaria), Limnos,Agios Kirykos (Ikaria), Mitilini (Lesvos) and Psara. Northern Greece: Kavala and Thessaloniki.
Confirm sailing times either with individual lines, the Greek/Hellenic National Tourist Organisation, Piraeus upon arrival Greece.
Routes from Rafina: There are local services from Rafina (near Athens) to: Amorgos, Andros, Agios Efstratios, Chalkida (summer only), Chios, Donoussa, Kavala, Koufonissi, Karistos (Evia), Kythnos, Limnos, Marmari (Evia), Milos, Mykonos, Heraklia, Naxos, Schinoussa, Serifos, Sifnos, Syros, Thessaloniki and Tinos.
Other routes: These include Agia Marina-Nea Styra; Perama-Salamis; Rio-Antirio; Eretria- Oropos; Patras-Ithaca; (Sami); Patras-Corfu; Patras-Paxi; Preveza-Aktion; Corfu-Paxi; Kyllini-Zante; Kyllini- Cephalonia (Poros); Kavala- Thassos (Prinos); Kavala-Thassos Alexandroupolis- Samothrace and Lavrion-Kea.
hydrofoil service (also called the Flying Dolphins) offers speedy and efficient service from Piraeus, travelling many the nearby islands. Though this somewhat more costly than travelling ferry, journey times are cut drastically. There are also fast hydrofoil services from Agios, Gytheion, Kimi (Evia), Konstandinos, Lavrion, Thessaloniki, Volos and Zea Marina (Piraeus).
For further information various ferry and hydrofoil timetables, visit www.gtp.gr.
Many types yachts and sailing vessels can chartered hired with without crews. 'Flotilla holidays' are popular, and the Greek/Hellenic National Tourism Organisation (see Contact Addresses section) has full list companies running these types holidays.
The two main railway stations Athens are Peloponnissos (with trains the Peloponnese)and Larissa (with trains northern Greece, Evia and Europe).
Information and tickets are available from the Hellenic Railways Organisation (OSE) Athens (tel: (210) 529 7313 529 7777) Thessaloniki (tel: (310) 599 143).
Travelling north, there are regular daily trains from Athens Thessaloniki, Livadia, Edessa, Florina, Seres, Larissa, Plati, Komotini and Alexandroupolis (connections from Thessaloniki and Larissa).
Travelling south, there are regular daily trains from Athens Kiato, Xylokastra, Diakofto, Tripoli, Patras, Olympia, Argos, Megalopolis and Kalamata.
Inter Rail Cards are open all European residents for unlimited rail travel 2nd class numerous European countries. Passes are valid for days month.
Euro Domino Cards are open passengers all ages and offer rail travel either 1st 2nd class travel one more European countries. Passes are valid for days and not have taken consecutively.
Vergina Flexipass offers unlimited rail travel Greece for days within months either first second class, depending the choice ticket.
Greek Flexipass offers unlimited rail travel Greece for days within month first class.Students may entitled reduction the price domestic rail fares. Travel limited certain routes and times. For more information the above schemes, contact the Hellenic Railways Organisation (OSE).
Greece has fine quality road network the whole, totalling approximately 116,150km (72,174 miles), mostly paved. Traffic drives the right.
Examples some distances from Athens: Corinth, 85km (53 miles); Igoumenitsa, 587km (365 miles); Thessaloniki, 511km (318 miles); and Delphi, 165km (103 miles).
Buses link Athens and all main towns northern Greece, Attica and the Peloponnese. Service the islands depends the demand, and timetables should checked carefully. Some islands not allow any kind motorised transport, which case islanders use boats, carts and donkeys travel around. Fares are low. The Greek/Hellenic Railways Organisation Ltd (OSE) runs bus services northern Greece from the Karolou Street terminus and the Peloponnese from the Sina Street station.
Bus information: There are two long-distance bus terminals Athens-Terminal and Terminal For detailed information long-distance buses, run KTEL, from Athens the provinces, enquire Terminal 100 Kifissou Street, Athens (tel: (210) 512 4910) Terminal 260 Liossion Street, Athens. Additional information can obtained from KTEL offices.
Rates are per and are very reasonable, with additional charge for fares to/from stations, ports and airports. Taxis run share basis, not surprised the taxi picks other passengers for the journey.
There additional charge from 0100-0600, with double fare from 0200-0400.
Most car hire firms operate throughout Greece. For details, contact the Greek/Hellenic National Tourism Organisation (see Contact Addresses section). Reservations can made either writing telephoning the car hire agency direct.
Minimum age for driving l8. Children under must sit the back seat. Seat belts must worn all times. There are fines for breaking traffic rules and regulations.
The maximum speed limit 120kph (70mph) motorways, 110kph (60mph) outside built-up areas and 50kph (31mph) built-up areas. There are different speed limits for motorbikes. against the law carry spare petrol the vehicle. nationals may import car, motorcycle, caravan, boat trailer for maximum six months. This period can extended months for fee and further paperwork.
national driving licence adequate for nationals. nationals that are taking their own cars Greece are advised obtain Green Card, top the insurance cover that provided the car owner's domestic policy. longer requirement law for visits less than months, but without insurance cover limited the minimum legal cover Greece. The car registration documents must carried all times. Nationals non-EU countries may need International Driving Permit and must contact ELPA (Automobile and Touring Club Greece).
breakdown service available main roads, conditions which have improved. For details, contact ELPA, Athens Tower, Messogion 2-4, 115 Athens (tel: (210) 779 1615; fax: (210) 778 6642; e-mail: Emergency breakdown services can contacted toll-free-dial 104. There are good repair shops big towns and petrol easily obtainable.
There are numerous services around Athens and Attica. The terminal Mauromateon Street, Areos Park, Athens has regular services Amfiaraio, Nea Makri, Porto Rafti, Marathonas, Ramnous and Sounio. Trolley buses ILPAP) and regular buses (ETHEL) have frequent links tourist sights and places interest. Tickets for buses and trolley buses can bought from the Athens Urban Transport Organisation (OASA) various booths and kiosks situated around the city. For more information contact OASA Metsovou Street, 106 Athens (tel: (210) 883 6076; fax: (210) 821 2219; e-mail: oasa@oasa.gr).
Athens has consistent underground system (ISAP) that consists three major lines. The old line runs north-south between Athens (suburb Kifissia) and Piraeus daily 0500-0015. There are also two new lines: Line runs between Aghios Antonios and Aghios Dimitrios and line runs between Monastiraki and the airport. Tickets can bought every Metro and ISAP station. Information timetables and schedules are available from Athens Metro OASA (see address details above).
new tram system Athens cuts through the city from Syntagma Square right through the coast and runs pleasurable route from Peace and Friendship Stadium all the way the most southern point Glyfada. Tickets can booked all stations and trams connect with the Metro Neos Kosmos and Neo Faliro (website: ww.tramsa.gr).

both inexpensive and easy travel around the islands. There are ferry services numerous routes, with sailings most frequent during the summer. The main ports are Attica, Rafina and Piraeus, although there are regular sailings the islands from the smaller ports Alexandroupolis, Igoumenitsa, Patras, Kavala, Kyllini, Thessaloniki and Volos.
Tickets can bought from the shipping lines' offices located around the quaysides. major ports the larger lines have offices the city centre. There are two classes ticket Economy Class and First Class) which offer varying degrees comfort; couchette cabins can booked for the longer voyages those wishing avoid the sun. Nearly all ships have restaurant facilities. During high season wise buy tickets well before hand, inter-island travel very popular.
Routes from Piraeus: There are regular sailings the following ports: Dodecanese: Astipalaia, Chalki, Karpathos, Kassos,Kalymnos, Kastelorizo, Kos, Leros, Lipsi, Nissiros, Patmos, Rhodes, Symi and Tilos. Cyclades: Aegiali and Katapola (both Amorgos), Anafi, Donoussa, Folegandros, Heraklia, Ios, Kimolos, Koufonissia, Kythnos, Paros, Milos, Mykonos, Naxos,Santorini, Schinoussa, Serifos, Sifnos, Sikinos, Siros and Tinos. Peloponnese: Kithira, Gytheion, Hermioni, Methana, Monemvassia and Porto Heli. Saronic Gulf Islands: Aegina, Hydra, Poros and Spetses. Crete: Heraklion, Kastelli, Agios Nikolaos, and Sitia. Samos: Karlovassi and Vathi. North Eastern Aegean Islands: Chios, Evdilos (Ikaria), Limnos,Agios Kirykos (Ikaria), Mitilini (Lesvos) and Psara. Northern Greece: Kavala and Thessaloniki.

Routes from Rafina: There are local services from Rafina (near Athens) to: Amorgos, Andros, Agios Efstratios, Chalkida (summer only), Chios, Donoussa, Kavala, Koufonissi, Karistos (Evia), Kythnos, Limnos, Marmari (Evia), Milos, Mykonos, Heraklia, Naxos, Schinoussa, Serifos, Sifnos, Syros, Thessaloniki and Tinos.
Other routes: These include Agia Marina-Nea Styra; Perama-Salamis; Rio-Antirio; Eretria- Oropos; Patras-Ithaca; (Sami); Patras-Corfu; Patras-Paxi; Preveza-Aktion; Corfu-Paxi; Kyllini-Zante; Kyllini- Cephalonia (Poros); Kavala- Thassos (Prinos); Kavala-Thassos Alexandroupolis- Samothrace and Lavrion-Kea.
hydrofoil service (also called the Flying Dolphins) offers speedy and efficient service from Piraeus, travelling many the nearby islands. Though this somewhat more costly than travelling ferry, journey times are cut drastically. There are also fast hydrofoil services from Agios, Gytheion, Kimi (Evia), Konstandinos, Lavrion, Thessaloniki, Volos and Zea Marina (Piraeus).
For further information various ferry and hydrofoil timetables, visit www.gtp.gr.
Many types yachts and sailing vessels can chartered hired with without crews. 'Flotilla holidays' are popular, and the Greek/Hellenic National Tourism Organisation (see Contact Addresses section) has full list companies running these types holidays.

Information and tickets are available from the Hellenic Railways Organisation (OSE) Athens (tel: (210) 529 7313 529 7777) Thessaloniki (tel: (310) 599 143).
Travelling north, there are regular daily trains from Athens Thessaloniki, Livadia, Edessa, Florina, Seres, Larissa, Plati, Komotini and Alexandroupolis (connections from Thessaloniki and Larissa).
Travelling south, there are regular daily trains from Athens Kiato, Xylokastra, Diakofto, Tripoli, Patras, Olympia, Argos, Megalopolis and Kalamata.

About Shape What and How Got Here.
Chris the CEO Shape. responsible for architecting and developing Shape's core platform. the past, has taken lead roles large scale software projects for marketing agencies and other tech companies like Intel. Chris brings that exceptional experience and technical skill, not mention his great attitude, the Shape team.
David co-founder and Senior Developer. talented full stack coder responsible for maintaining the site and developing new features. attended Oregon State University pursuing degree Computer Science before jumping into the startup world full time. When isn't working Shape enjoys hanging out with his girlfriend and rooting for his favorite athlete Novak Djokovic.
Dominic the Engineering Shape. joined the founders through series very fortunate encounters Bend, Oregon. His focus constantly shifts wherever the fire hottest. commands the front end the Shape platform, directs engineering resources the back end, and provides design work for the sales and marketing team. Outside work, loves explore the world's places with good friends and family.
Jon co-founder Shape. spent years PPC consultant, agency analyst and department manager before leaving build useful apps for other PPC analysts. The origins Shape come from problems Jon saw first hand the industry. focuses product direction, customer service and sales for our company, reach out him with any questions jon@shape.io.
Maxwel the 'jack all trades' the Shape office working Sales Development, Customer Service, Client Success, Digital Marketing, and Culture Appreciation. graduate Oregon State University living Bend, Oregon, Max brings load digital marketing and sales experience the team. not the Shape office, Max likes long rides Mt. Bachelor, good game his hands, and time well spent with those cares about.
Nicole the Marketing Communications Shape. She has extensive background the PPC industry working analyst, department head and product manager. Nicole's focus crafting our website, marketing materials and support documents clearly communicate how PPC professionals can benefit from our platform. When she's not working, she enjoys hiking with her husband and half golden retriever/half tornado (Parker), baking, and reading.
Tyler Business Development Shape. comes from years analyst, account manager and sales consultant focused the PPC industry. was responsible for millions dollars client spend his agency job Shape customer. Tyler excited use his experience help relate the challenges that face PPC analysts and how Shape can help. When isn't working enjoys golfing and playing with his dog Carl.
Our founding team's experience digital marketing lends perspective product decisions that especially empathetic the day-to-day challenges faced our customers. This the product always wished existed.
We've spent lot time manually updating Excel spreadsheets and Google Docs. analyst's time valuable. Time should spent the fun challenges being analyst: the optimization leading improvement performance. Analysts know the more time spent optimizing the better for everyone.
Shape team developers, designers and analysts with the goal serving digital marketing professionals and the organizations they run.
love hearing ideas from analysts about how software could make their lives better. you have idea for improvement, suggestion for Shape, please reach out. View our Contact Page for more details about how get touch.

original text:
University California's Cooperative Extension and the California Cotton Ginners and Growers Associations have partnered host four regional whitefly/Sticky Cotton meetings within the Central Valley. Discussions Dr. Peter Goodell, Dr. Larry Godfrey and Dr. Bob Hutmacher will include information regarding sugar deposits cotton lint and the following damage/loss production, monitoring and management assessment aphid and whiteflies, review Best Management Practices including insecticide selection and the managing late season cotton. addition County Commissioners will discuss the current status new Chlorpyrifos regulations, critical product whitefly and aphid pest control. PCA units have been requested and are pending. Meeting locations times are Thursday, July 16th 9-11 a.m. San Joaquin Valley Quality Cotton Growers Association office Shafter and 12-3 County Line Gin Hanford, along with Friday, July 17th a.m.-12 p.m. Westside Farmers Co-Op Gin Tranquillity and 3-5 p.m. West Side REC Five Points. Contact Shana Colby RSVP (559)252-0684 email shana@ccgga.org.
This entry was posted NEWS ISSUES July 2015 ccgga.
